title: Why Two Electrons Avoid Each Other With No Force

metadata.color_law: TEAL = antisymmetric spatial state (parallel spins, node at coincidence, lower energy); CRIMSON = symmetric spatial state (opposite spins, enhanced overlap, higher energy)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no exchange-integral J/K formulas on screen, no Slater-determinant algebra, no spin-statistics-theorem proof
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B14: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Give two electrons parallel spins and they keep their distance — even though nothing in the equations pushes them apart.

card.title: Why Two Electrons Avoid Each Other With No Force

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Give two electrons parallel spins and they keep their distance — even though nothing in the equations pushes them apart.

beats.act: COLD OPEN — concrete case (oxygen is magnetic)
beats.narration_text: Oxygen has two electrons in its outer shell with the same spin direction. They spread apart, lower the atom's energy, and make oxygen a magnet.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Oxygen has two electrons in its outer shell with the same spin direction. They spread apart, lower the atom's energy, and make oxygen a magnet.

beats.narration_text: The electrons feel no special repulsion from spin. The Hamiltonian has no spin-spin force term. Something else is keeping them apart.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The electrons feel no special repulsion from spin. The Hamiltonian has no spin-spin force term. Something else is keeping them apart.

beats.narration_text: Parallel-spin electrons sit farther apart and cost less energy. No force pushes them. Why does their spin direction control their separation?

card.title: Spin controls separation. No force does it. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Parallel-spin electrons sit farther apart and cost less energy. No force pushes them. Why does their spin direction control their separation?

beats.narration_text: You might guess spin-up and spin-down repel each other, like magnets. But electrons' magnetic moments are tiny — too weak to move them apart at atomic scales.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: You might guess spin-up and spin-down repel each other, like magnets. But electrons' magnetic moments are tiny — too weak to move them apart at atomic scales.

beats.narration_text: The real constraint is deeper: two electrons have no individual identity. When their clouds overlap there is no fact about which is which — only one combined wavefunction.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The real constraint is deeper: two electrons have no individual identity. When their clouds overlap there is no fact about which is which — only one combined wa

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — antisymmetry forces a node
beats.narration_text: Fermions need an antisymmetric wavefunction. For parallel spins the spin part is symmetric, so the spatial part must be antisymmetric — and that function must equal zero wherever both electrons coincide.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Fermions need an antisymmetric wavefunction. For parallel spins the spin part is symmetric, so the spatial part must be antisymmetric — and that function must e

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — the joint probability map
beats.narration_text: Map joint probability over the two positions. Parallel spins carve a node along the diagonal — electrons can never be at the same spot. Opposite spins pile up there instead.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Map joint probability over the two positions. Parallel spins carve a node along the diagonal — electrons can never be at the same spot. Opposite spins pile up t

beats.narration_text: Farther apart means less Coulomb repulsion. Less repulsion means lower energy. The antisymmetric state is cheaper — no force required, just geometry of the wavefunction.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Farther apart means less Coulomb repulsion. Less repulsion means lower energy. The antisymmetric state is cheaper — no force required, just geometry of the wave

beats.act: THE IMPLICATION — Hund's rule and oxygen
beats.narration_text: This is Hund's rule: fill each orbital with one parallel-spin electron before pairing. The exchange effect stabilizes the high-spin state. That is why oxygen is paramagnetic.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Hund's rule: fill each orbital with one parallel-spin electron before pairing. The exchange effect stabilizes the high-spin state. That is why oxygen is

beats.narration_text: Suppose you have two electrons in a one-dimensional box, parallel spins. Their joint probability map has a node on the diagonal — they're never found at the same position. Flip one spin, the node disappears, they bunch up, energy rises by roughly 0.80 eV in helium.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Suppose you have two electrons in a one-dimensional box, parallel spins. Their joint probability map has a node on the diagonal — they're never found at the sam

beats.narration_text: Parallel-spin electrons avoid each other because antisymmetry carves a node at coincidence — less Coulomb energy, no force needed. That is the exchange effect.

card.title: Antisymmetry carves a node. No force needed.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Parallel-spin electrons avoid each other because antisymmetry carves a node at coincidence — less Coulomb energy, no force needed. That is the exchange effect.
